sentences.text: Freda Isaksen, F-R-E-D-A I-S-A-K-S-E-N.
Freda Trachtenberg.
I was born July the 9th, 1914, so I'm 81 years old.
In London, England.
No.
My parents were from Russia, or maybe even Poland, because the borders used to change so much in those days-- so it's either Russia or Poland.
Yes.
Partly, really not so much because of persecution, I think.
My father was one of a twin, and his father was ill and wanted to go to London for-- to be-- to get some kind of medical treatment.
My father went with him, and so my father never returned back to Russia.
He stayed in England.
Well, I think he had two or three occupations during the years, but he-- finally, he was a manufacturer of cardboard boxes.
No.
Yes, I had three sisters and one brother.
Minnie, Rose, Phyllis, and my brother Jack.
Oh, yes.
I had a lot of my aunts and uncles from both my mother and my father.
And I had a lot of cousins, and I was very closely attached-- attached to my cousins.
I lived in Stamford Hill.
We had our own home.
Stamford Hill is in the north of London.
Well, you know, the English houses are quite different.
Our house was semi-detached, and it goes up several floors.
You have one or two rooms on each floor, so the house is about four, at least four storeys high, with a basement.
Oh yes, we had a maid, uh-huh.
No boarders.
Yes.
Her name was Emily.
Well, I guess one of them.
The maid I remember mostly was named Emily, and she lived with us until she was an old lady.
We were orthodox.
Not ultra orthodox, but orthodox.
Stamford Hill Synagogue, which was right within walking distance from our home.
Oh, yes.
I couldn't say how many at that time, but there were a lot of Jewish people lived in London, and still do, of course.
There was--
I never encountered any-- any hostility.
Nothing whatsoever.
My-- my family were very anxious that I should disassociate with Jewish friends, which I did, mostly.
But I never encountered hostility in the neighborhood or in the schools.
Oh, yes, Friday night was very important.
My mother would make a real Shabbos dinner and light the candles.
My father had his own business, and Friday was a busy day for him because he had to do the payroll.
And in those days, the payroll was all in cash.
So he would come home very late, and we were not allowed to have dinner until my father came home.
That was-- they were very strict about that.
And then when he came home, we had to daven, so sometimes it could be as late as 10-- 10 or 11:00 o'clock at night before we were allowed to have dinner.
And then he always brought home a bag of peanuts on a Friday night, and so after the dinner, we would sit around the fire-- the open fire-- and crack the peanuts.
Yes, I did.
Well, I always liked journalism.
I think I would have liked to have become a journalist.
That was my favorite subject-- writing-- always has been and is until this day.
But I was never able to become a journalist.
In those days, educating a girl wasn't all that important.
My brother was the one who got the higher education.
They all used to say, well, you'll end up washing the diapers anyway.
Well, let me explain to you the English public school system-- which was absolutely terrible.
I think it has changed a little.
At 10 years old, every student would take an exam.
If they passed the exam satisfactorily, they could go on to school until 16.
If they did not pass too well, then the compulsory education was until 14.
Fortunately, I passed the exam, and so I went on to public school until 16.
And then I really didn't know I could do very much, so my parents had me go to a school for dress designing.
So I learned to be a cutter and designer.
I only went there for two years.
So that was the extent of my education.
Oh, yes, and I continued with that, and always-- always loved-- I used to make my own clothes, and always loved to do that.
Just for about three years, when I met my husband.
And then that was the end of that.
Well, I met him in London.

My husband was-- had come to London also to attend a cutting and designing school, because he was in the-- actually, he was in the gentleman's outfitting business, and he had been trained as a master tailor.
There's quite a little story to that if we have time to tell it.
His family owned seven stores in the small town of Trondheim, and they sewed gentlemen's wear-- right from boots to shirts and complete outfitting.
There were nine brothers, and he was second from the youngest, and they decided at least one of them should know what they were doing.
They were all businessmen, and they didn't understand the actual technical work of it at all.
So he learned to become a master tailor, which later on saved his life.
Well, maybe we'll get to that later.
So, he went to England to learn-- after-- he-- he had already become a master tailor.
So he went to England to perfect his work.
Then I met him in England-- in London.
How long did you know him before you were married?
Well, that's quite a story, too.
My girlfriend and I wondered what to do one Saturday night, and we looked in the which is comparable to the here.
And we went to this dance at Boots Cafe-- well, it was actually above Boots Pharmacy on Regent Street in London, and that's where I met my husband.
Well, it's a longer story than that, but I'm not going to go into all that--the long story, I don't think.
I met him there, and we started to date.
And then he went back to Norway, and he just casually asked me if I would come to Norway for a holiday.
I thought that would be fun, not thinking that I would end up marrying him.
I thought that it would be fun to go to Norway.
So I went there at the beginning of the summer, and I stayed with his family a few weeks, and then we decided to get married.
So actually, I know-- only knew him for just a few weeks, because we got married in September of that same year.
No, when I was in Norway and we decided to get married, and I wrote and told my parents that we'd decided to get married, and they say, a nice Jewish girl gets married from her own home.
So no way are you going to get married in Norway.
And they said, we have booked your wedding for September the 13th.
So I went back to England, made arrangements for the wedding, and then he-- he came and we got married in London.
Did you live in Norway after that?
Oh yes.
We lived-- we lived in Trondheim, Norway.
And never dreamed of leaving.
It was because my husband had his own business, and there was no reason why we had to emigrate-- until the Nazis came and that was settled it.
No, it was not a difficult adjustment, because my husband had a wonderful family and I always got along with them very well.
And they welcomed me with open arms, and I had a very good life there.
I learnt the language very fast.
When I first heard the Norwegian language, I thought, I'm never in the world going to learn this.
It sounded like gibberish.
But I took lessons and I learned the language.
Within six months, I could converse and-- and had no problem with the language.
The climate-- well, it's a little worse than it was in England.
Just much colder.
But I-- I adjusted very well.
No, we never-- we didn't have our own house.
We lived in an apartment.
It's not -- well, it's different to here.
They don't have big apartment houses.
No, I never-- never did.
I helped my husband in his business.
I've never worked outside of the home since I married.
Well, from 1933 on, of course [NON-ENGLISH] didn't get there until 1936-- yeah, we had all the news of what was going on in Germany, and we knew things were very bad for the-- for the Jewish people.
So we were-- not only Germany, but of course, we-- you see, Norway being a small country, always so concerned with politics, and everything, in other countries around them.
So we were well aware of everything, but we never dreamed-- never dreamed-- until the night of April the 9th that Norway would be invaded.
There was never any thought of it.
Yes.
We went-- we were tired, went to bed as we usually did.
And then, during the night, we heard planes flying overhead.
And I mean-- the planes, we thought, what on earth is going on?
So many planes.
So we turned on the radio, and we heard that Germany had invaded Norway.

So we immediately called my husband's brother, who lives in the-- lived in the central part of the town, and he said there were Nazis, there were Germans, wearing swastikas walking through the streets.
So we knew what had happened.
So the next morning, the whole family gathered.
Remember, they were a huge family-- nine brothers-- 10 brothers and sisters, nine boys and two girls, and we all met at his mother's house.
And what are we going to do?
So we decided-- it was decided that we would leave right away.
There were seven stores there.
They closed the stores, and we didn't even go into the stores that morning.
And decided-- there were several children, three or four of the brothers were married, I think there were two single-- with two single brothers.
They decided that we would all go, except the single men.
The single men would stay and try to join the army.
Because we thought, well, England is never going to allow Germany to stay in Norway.
They're going to come and rescue us.
We were so sure that this was just going to be a temporary thing.
Well, my mother-in-law did not want to go.
She had lived in Poland and emigrated about 1900s-- emigrate-- emigrated to Norway because of persecution.
Now she says, I'm not going a second time.
She says, this is my home, this is where I'm going to stay.
She said she's going to stay.
And then her daughter says, well, I'm staying with my mother.
And I was pregnant, and I didn't want to go without my husband.
No-- my husband, he was young, and he was brave and he said, I'm not going either.
I'm going to go join the army and I'm going to fight for Norway.
So I said, well, I'm not going without my husband.
So the mother-- the married brothers all left with their families and their children.
They took the train to Orland, which is on the border of Sweden.
And Sweden, you know, allows-- Sweden and Denmark and Norway, they allow the citizens to travel, so you don't have to have visas or passports.
And they left.
Well, my mother-in-law and my sister-in-law and I stayed there three days.
The husbands, they went off to try and join the army.
And after three days, we realized this was impossible.
I was afraid to open my mouth because I was British-- British and Jewish, what a terrible combination in Norway.
And they were scared to walk out of the house, so we decided we'd better go.
We'd better follow the rest of the family.
Soldiers, that's all.
There was no fighting.
There was no fighting, and there was no mention of touching the Jewish people.
No, there was nothing going on.
It's just that people were scared.
So, we got on the train-- my mother-in-law, my sister-in-law, and I left the men behind, the single men and my husband.
And we took the train and left.
And we did not know that that was the last train ever allowed out of Norway.
That was the last train, that the Germans stopped all trains going-- all trains to cross the border.
All you could do was get to the border.
So we did not know that was the last train, so we just took the last train out.

And after some days-- or actually it was a week, I don't remember exactly, it might have been weeks-- my husband and his brothers realized that there was not going to be any Norwegian army fighting against the Germans.
There was no such thing.
So they skied-- they got on skis, and they skied right across Norway to the border.
It took them several days.
But actually, that was a very dangerous thing to do, because the border was patrolled, and when they got close to the border they saw these troops skiing down-- it was the middle of winter.
They saw these troops, and they didn't know if they were Germans or Swedish or-- well, they knew they wouldn't be Norwegian.
And they thought, well, if they're Germans, that's the end of us.
They'll just shoot them, because they had done that to others.
They just shoot them right there at the border, if they're trying to cross.
But then they saw the Swedish uniforms, and the Swedish soldiers came down and escorted them across the border.
It was the Spring of 1940, uh-huh.
April-- well, it was, yeah, the spring, April of 1940.
Yes, well, with the whole family, there were 21 of us-- we stayed in this old house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a very old house, it was halfway abandoned.
And we didn't have beds, the plumbing didn't work, but we thought that we really-- our humor was not bad, because we really thought that the British were going to come and get rid of the Germans.
We really thought so.
So we were just kind of waiting.
We were just sitting in Halldale, with very little money, because all they'd taken was petty cash-- they hadn't taken-- were not able to take money out of the banks.
They'd just taken petty cash.
And we just waited.
And then we finally realized, the British are not going to come.
The British are not going to come to save Norway.
So we thought, oh, what are we doing here in Halldale?
We'd better move to the city.
So we got-- all got on the train again and we moved to Stockholm.
And we moved into a place in Stockholm.
Now, the Swedish government had moved-- they'd transferred the king to Stockholm.
And they were very anxious to help their own citizens, so they helped us find a place to live.
And we lived in Stockholm for a while.
We did receive a little, yes.
We did receive a little.
And my husband did find work, because he had a trade.
You see, that's what saved his life.
His brothers couldn't do anything-- they were businessmen.
They always had been successful businessmen, but you just don't go out and start a business when you come to Stockholm like that.
My husband was the one who had found work.
I had packed this one suitcase with clothing, and I had-- we had a lot of silver, and I had put silver in the suitcase.
Because I knew that would be valuable.
That's what I had.
Myself?
I didn't work.
None of my sisters-in-law worked.
We were-- how many sisters-in-law were there?
Half a dozen of us.
And we just kept the house, kept the place going.
And as refugees started to cross the border, they all knew-- it became known-- they used to go, say, well, how are we going to cross into-- well, what are we going to do when we get there?
And it became known that you go to the Isaksens.
And the refugees used to come to us.
This was a large apartment we had-- well, we were 21 people.
We used to sleep on the floor.
And they used to come to us, and we would put everybody up.
We would put them on the floor, and they would stay with us.
And there were-- oh, dozens of people every day at our house, at our apartment.
Because we became well-known for that.
Go to the-- go to the Isaksens.
From Norway.
From Norway.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish-- Jewish people from Norway.
Some of them, there were a couple that got shot on the border.
And I remember one fellow that was staying with us.
Well, let me tell you something-- what happened with these Norwegian refugees.
They were all naive.
They were all naive.
They came to Stockholm, and at that time, the Germans were not doing anything to the Jewish people.
Of course we kept, you know-- because In Stockholm, you were allowed to use a shortwave radio.
So we could know what was going on in the rest of the world.
The Germans were not doing anything to the Norwegian Jews, and they were naive enough to think that it would continue that way.
What-- and so, they wanted to go back.
Remember, these were all fairly prosperous people.
Most of the Norwegian Jews were businessmen-- had had their own prosperous businesses.
And here they were in Stockholm with no way of making a living.
And so they wanted to go back, which is the most idiotic, most foolish thing.
It seems to me that I was the only person that didn't want to go back.
There may have been a reason for that.
One reason for that may have been that-- remember, I was British-born.
My roots were not actually in Norway, and I had no family in Norway.
I had no personal family.
You see, these refugees were mostly people who had left mothers and fathers and family behind in Norway, and they were terribly anxious for their family.
Maybe that's the reason why I was the only person who didn't want to go back.
They were so naive, they thought the Germans would not disturb them, and a lot of them went back-- including my husband's own family.
So in the end, it was just me and my husband left.
Not really.
My husband was a very adventurous type of person, always was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] an adventurer.
He went to Germany before he was married, he went to England.
And he-- he said, no, if I didn't want to go back, he wouldn't go back.
And we had a son born in Stockholm.
And he said that he was not going to go back with me-- without me-- and there was no way that I was going to go back.
So unfortunately, my husband's family returned.
And, well, we all know what happened to the Jewish people.
Some of them escaped again.
Some of them escaped again by skiing across the border the second time.
Some of them were sent to Auschwitz.
My husband's family-- women, children.
Yeah.

Two of his brothers, after they returned to Trondheim, were sitting in their home and listening to the radio, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I don't know if they were reported, or how the soldiers came to break into their homes and found them listening to the radio.
They took them out, took them-- arrested them, put them in Kristiansten, which is a fortress in Trondheim, and shot them.
My mother-in-law was in Stockholm.
The reason she didn't go back-- she would have gone back, too, but her daughter became sick, and was in the hospital.
So she didn't go back to Trondheim, because she was staying with her-- she stayed with her daughter.
And she was sitting, listening to-- to the radio in Stockholm, and heard the announcement that her two sons had been shot.
See they were-- because they had monopolized the business in Trondheim, of gentlemen's wear, they were a fairly well-known family.
And they announced over the radio that her two sons had been shot.
And my mother-in-law heard that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, nothing more to be said.
Yeah, some of them were sent to Auschwitz.
Three, four little children.
Four small children.
Two brothers and their wives went to Auschwitz, and some of them escaped.
I can say that they escaped with a little help of the Norwegian, non-Jewish people.
They hid them.

One sister-in-law-- the sister-in-law whose husband was shot, she took the two children, one child on her back, on skis, crossed the-- crossed across-- you know that's-- I'm talking about crossing a whole country.
If you know the-- if you've ever looked at a map of Norway, Norway's a very narrow country.
Trondheim is on the coast.
She skied right across with a child in her back-- Adella.
Yes.
Sweden was very peaceful, but it was like sitting on a keg of dynamite.
You didn't know, from one day to another, if the Germans could invade.
You see, Sweden allowed German troops to travel across.
That's well-known.
In order to get their troops across, to-- the fastest way would be to travel across Sweden.
And Sweden consented to this.
Not because they wanted to-- well, I'm not going to go into politics, of course-- not because they wanted to, but because if they hadn't, Germany would have just gone in and taken the whole country.
So they allowed the German troops to travel across into Norway.
And the thought was, if Germany kept on demanding things of Sweden, Sweden would do it.
And so, what would happen?
We didn't know-- we didn't know where we were from day to day.
So, as I say, my husband was a very adventurous person.
He said, I want to go to the United States.
He didn't ever thought to live in the United States for the rest of his life, just until the war's over.
And so, that's what we did.
We applied to the United States for a visa.
At first, they refused-- they didn't grant us a visa, but then later on, they wrote to us and said they would-- It was a little politics.
It was before the elections, and they weren't making any changes or something, so, anyway, they did grant us visas.
Well, remember, we only lived in Sweden for one-- I'm talking about 50 years ago.
Everything's not so clear in my mind.
But we only lived in Sweden for about a year, so it may have taken, oh, half a year before they granted us the visas.
Oh, it was soon-- I remember, it was soon after the presidential elections that year, and the presidential elections are always in November.
So it must have been soon after November.
So maybe it took six months.
Well, that was the difficult thing.
We-- we decided to go to the United States, and we hadn't-- well, we thought we'd go to the United States the way everybody used to go to the United States.
You just cross the Atlantic.
In those days, remember, people did not fly the way they do today.
We thought you just fly across the Atlantic and go to New York.
